section.title: The Year of the Lord's Favor
section.sectionRationale: This is the passage Jesus stands and reads in the synagogue at Nazareth, then rolls up the scroll and says, today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing, Luke 4:16-21. He stops mid-sentence, before the day of our God's vengeance, a deliberate pause that shapes how the whole chapter, and the two advents of Christ, must be read.
section.thesis: The Spirit-anointed Servant brings good news to the poor, freedom to captives, and comfort to mourners, inaugurating the year of the LORD's favor now in His first coming, while the day of vengeance He named but did not yet enact awaits His return.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Spirit's anointing and the Servant's mission; rebuilding, double honor, and an everlasting covenant; and the Servant's own rejoicing, robed in salvation as a bridegroom and bride.

units.label: The Spirit of the Lord GOD is on Me

verses.text: The Spirit of the Lord GOD is on Me, because the LORD has {{mashach|anointed}} Me to preach good news to the poor. He has sent Me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim liberty to the captives and freedom to the prisoners,
to proclaim the {{ratzon|year of the LORD’s favor}} and the day of our God’s vengeance, to comfort all who mourn,

to console the mourners in Zion, to give them a crown of beauty for ashes, the oil of joy for mourning, and a garment of praise for a spirit of despair. So they will be called oaks of righteousness, the planting of the LORD, that He may be glorified.

units.label: Rebuild, restore, a double portion
units.note: priests of the LORD, an everlasting covenant

verses.text: They will rebuild the ancient ruins; they will restore the places long devastated; they will renew the ruined cities, the desolations of many generations.
Strangers will stand and feed your flocks, and foreigners will be your plowmen and vinedressers.
But you will be called the priests of the LORD; they will speak of you as ministers of our God; you will feed on the wealth of nations, and you will boast in their riches.

Instead of shame, My people will have a double portion, and instead of humiliation, they will rejoice in their share; and so they will inherit a double portion in their land, and everlasting joy will be theirs.
For I, the LORD, love justice; I hate robbery and iniquity; in My faithfulness I will give them their recompense and make an everlasting covenant with them.
Their descendants will be known among the nations, and their offspring among the peoples. All who see them will acknowledge that they are a people the LORD has blessed.

units.label: Clothed with garments of salvation
units.note: a bridegroom, a bride, righteousness springing up

verses.text: I will rejoice greatly in the LORD, my soul will exult in my God; for He has clothed me with garments of salvation and wrapped me in a robe of righteousness, as a bridegroom wears a priestly headdress, as a bride adorns herself with her jewels.
For as the earth brings forth its growth, and as a garden enables seed to spring up, so the Lord GOD will cause righteousness and praise to spring up before all the nations.

mashach.gloss: To anoint, to consecrate by pouring oil, the verb behind the noun Mashiach, Messiah, the Anointed One. The Speaker is anointed by the Spirit for a specific mission, good news, healing, liberty, exactly the mission Jesus claims for Himself when He reads this passage and says, today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing, Luke 4:21.

ratzon.gloss: The year of favor or acceptance, likely evoking the Year of Jubilee, Leviticus 25, when debts were forgiven, slaves freed, and land restored. Jesus stops His reading of this verse exactly at favor, before the day of vengeance, deliberately marking His first coming as the proclamation of favor, with the day of vengeance reserved for His return.

basic.context: This is the passage Jesus reads aloud in His hometown synagogue and then declares fulfilled in Himself, Luke 4:16-21. A Spirit-anointed figure announces good news to the poor, freedom for captives, and comfort for mourners, in language that recalls the Year of Jubilee, a season of release and restoration.
basic.hermeneutics: Jesus' own use of this text is the interpretive key: He reads up through comfort all who mourn and stops there, closing the scroll before the day of our God's vengeance. This deliberate pause teaches that His first coming inaugurates the favor while the vengeance awaits His second coming, a two-advent structure the church has always recognized.
basic.originalLanguages: The Speaker is mashach, anointed, by the Spirit, the very word behind Messiah. He proclaims the shenat ratzon, the year of favor, evoking the Jubilee's release of captives and restoration of land, a season of grace Jesus announces as beginning in Himself.
basic.typology: The anointed Herald preaching good news to the poor and binding up the brokenhearted anticipates and is fulfilled in Christ's earthly ministry. The double portion and everlasting covenant for a once-shamed people anticipate the church's inheritance in Christ. The garments of salvation and robe of righteousness anticipate the wedding imagery of Christ and His bride in Revelation 19.
basic.christ: Jesus reads this exact passage in Luke 4:16-21 and declares, today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing, applying the Spirit's anointing and the mission of good news, liberty, and comfort directly to Himself, while deliberately stopping before the day of vengeance, since His first coming brings grace, and His second coming will bring final judgment.
basic.god: God is both merciful and just, and this chapter holds the two together without confusion, He loves justice and hates robbery and iniquity, verse 8, even while proclaiming a year of favor to the brokenhearted and captive; His justice is not canceled by His mercy, it is simply not yet fully unveiled.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 4:16-21 has Jesus read this passage and apply it directly to Himself. Leviticus 25 describes the Year of Jubilee this passage evokes. Revelation 19:7-8 pictures the bride clothed for her wedding, echoing verse 10's bridegroom and bride imagery.
basic.oneStory: A Spirit-anointed figure was promised who would announce a year of favor, release captives, and comfort mourners, stopping short of final judgment until a later day. Jesus stands in a synagogue, reads these very words, and says they are fulfilled in Him, marking His first coming as the opening of grace and His return as the day this chapter also names but does not yet unleash.

context.literary: A single, unified prophetic announcement in the Servant's own voice, moving from His Spirit-given mission, through the community's restored fortunes, to His own personal rejoicing in garments of salvation, closing the Book of Comfort's central Servant material on a note of jubilant celebration.
context.historical: Addressed to a community still living amid ruin and devastation, promised not only rebuilding but honor among the nations and a covenant that would never be revoked, language deliberately evoking the periodic release and restoration of the Jubilee year, Leviticus 25.
context.canonical: Becomes the single most explicit self-identification of Jesus' mission in the Gospels, Luke 4:16-21, where He reads this text in His hometown synagogue and announces its fulfillment in Himself, making it one of the clearest hinges between Old Testament promise and New Testament fulfillment in the whole Bible.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce a Spirit-anointed figure whose mission would bring genuine liberation and comfort to the broken and oppressed, framed in the joyous, restorative language of Jubilee, while also naming a future day of divine reckoning against injustice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is prophetic proclamation in the first person of the Anointed One's own mission; it prescribes a response of receiving this good news, verse 1-3, rather than commanding specific behavior, though it does reveal what a restored, righteous community should look like, verses 4-9.

debates.question: Why does Jesus stop reading before 'the day of our God's vengeance' in Luke 4:19?

views.label: His first coming is exclusively about grace, with judgment entirely deferred
views.case: Jesus' deliberate stopping point signals that His earthly ministry is characterized wholly by favor, healing, and release, leaving final judgment for a separate, future moment tied to His second coming.

views.label: Both are true realities, unfolding in two comings
views.case: Jesus does not deny the day of vengeance exists, only that it was not the content of that day's fulfillment; the whole chapter remains true, favor proclaimed at His first coming, vengeance reserved for His return, 2 Thessalonians 1:7-10.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Jesus' pause is deliberate and meaningful: the year of the LORD's favor is now open in Him, while the day of vengeance He named but did not yet enact will be fulfilled at His second coming, a two-advent pattern the New Testament consistently teaches.

study.originalLanguages: The Speaker is mashach, anointed by the Spirit for a specific mission, the very word behind Mashiach, Messiah. He proclaims the shenat ratzon, the year of favor, deliberately evoking the Jubilee's release of debts, slaves, and land, Leviticus 25, a season of grace Jesus claims as beginning in His own ministry.

typology.type: The Spirit-anointed Herald of good news, liberty, and comfort, verses 1-3
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, who reads this text of Himself and inaugurates its fulfillment, Luke 4:16-21.

typology.type: The year of the LORD's favor, verse 2
typology.fulfillment: The present age of grace opened at Christ's first coming, 2 Corinthians 6:2, now is the favorable time.

typology.type: Garments of salvation, a bridegroom and bride, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: The marriage of the Lamb and His bride clothed in righteous deeds, Revelation 19:7-8.

study.christ: Jesus stands in the Nazareth synagogue, unrolls the scroll of Isaiah to this exact passage, reads it aloud, and says, today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing, Luke 4:16-21, the clearest self-identification of His mission anywhere in the Gospels. He deliberately stops mid-sentence, before the day of our God's vengeance, because His first coming proclaims favor while His second coming will bring the reckoning this chapter also names, 2 Thessalonians 1:7-10. And the garments of salvation and bridegroom imagery of verse 10 anticipate the marriage supper of the Lamb, where Christ's bride, the church, is clothed in righteous deeds, Revelation 19:7-8.
study.god: God is faithful to His covenant, verse 8, loving justice while hating the robbery and iniquity His people have suffered, and He is generous beyond calculation, promising a double portion of honor to a people who knew only shame, an everlasting covenant that will never again be revoked.

crossReferences.note: Jesus reads this exact passage and declares it fulfilled in Himself.
The Year of Jubilee, releasing debts, slaves, and land, the background for the year of the LORD's favor.
Paul echoes now is the favorable time, now is the day of salvation, applying this chapter's grace to the present gospel age.
The bride of the Lamb clothed in righteous deeds, echoing verse 10's bridal imagery.

study.oneStory: A Spirit-anointed figure was promised who would proclaim a year of grace, favor, and release to the broken and oppressed, while a day of final reckoning waited beyond it. Jesus reads these very words in His hometown synagogue and says they are fulfilled in Him, opening the year of the LORD's favor that still stands open today, with the day of vengeance reserved for His certain return.

sermon.title: Hope and Renewal: The Prophetic Vision of Zion's Restoration in Isaiah
sermon.why: Carson reads and expounds Isaiah 61:1 to 63:14 in this address, giving sustained attention to how Isaiah 61:1-2 is quoted by Jesus in Luke 4 and echoed in Matthew 11, tracing the passage's fulfillment in Christ's proclaimed mission of good news to the poor.

quiz.q: What happens when Jesus reads this passage in Luke 4:16-21?

options.text: He reads through comfort all who mourn, stops before the day of vengeance, and declares the Scripture fulfilled in Himself.
options.note: Luke 4:20-21 records Jesus' deliberate stopping point and His declaration of fulfillment.

options.text: He reads the entire passage including the day of vengeance and declares it all fulfilled that day.
options.note: He actually stops deliberately before the day of vengeance, a detail with real theological significance for how His first coming differs from His second.

options.text: He reads it but says it refers only to a future era, not to Himself.
options.note: He explicitly says, today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.

options.text: The Gospels never record Jesus reading from Isaiah in a synagogue.

quiz.q: What background does 'the year of the LORD's favor' most likely evoke?

options.text: The Year of Jubilee, when debts were forgiven, slaves freed, and land restored, Leviticus 25.
options.note: The language of liberty, release, and restoration closely matches Jubilee provisions.

options.text: An annual harvest festival with no connection to release or freedom.
options.note: The passage's emphasis on freedom for captives and prisoners points to Jubilee, not merely harvest themes.
The imagery is about release and restoration, not military triumph.
The weekly Sabbath shares the rhythm of rest, but the larger, cyclical release of the Jubilee year is the clearer background for this specific language.

quiz.q: Why is it significant that Jesus stops reading before 'the day of our God's vengeance'?

options.text: It shows His first coming proclaims grace and favor, while the day of vengeance awaits His second coming.
options.note: This reflects the New Testament's two-advent pattern, grace now, judgment at Christ's return.

options.text: It shows Jesus rejected the idea of any future judgment.
options.note: Other New Testament texts affirm a future day of judgment tied to Christ's return.

options.text: It shows the day of vengeance had already occurred before His ministry.
options.note: This misreads the timing; the vengeance is still future in New Testament teaching, tied to Christ's return, not something already past.

options.text: It has no theological significance and is simply where the reading happened to end.
options.note: The stopping point is widely understood as deliberate and theologically meaningful.

quiz.q: What image describes the Servant's own joy in verse 10?

options.text: Being clothed with garments of salvation and a robe of righteousness, like a bridegroom and bride adorned for a wedding.
options.note: Verse 10 uses explicit bridal and priestly imagery for the Servant's joy.

options.text: Being stripped of all honor and left in mourning clothes.
options.note: The opposite; the imagery is of celebratory adornment, not mourning.

options.text: Receiving a military crown after conquest.
options.note: It rightly senses triumph, but the imagery given is bridal and priestly, not militaristic.

options.text: Sitting silently with no outward expression of joy.
options.note: The verse explicitly says, I will rejoice greatly, and my soul will exult.

questions: Jesus claimed this passage as His own mission statement, good news to the poor, freedom to captives, comfort to mourners. Where in your life do you need to receive that good news rather than just admire it as someone else's story?

Jesus stopped reading before the day of vengeance, living now in the year of favor. How does knowing you live in the age of grace, not yet final judgment, shape how you treat people who are still far from God?
Verse 3 promises a crown of beauty for ashes, oil of joy for mourning. What ashes are you currently sitting in that you have not yet brought to God for that exchange?
The passage promises double honor to a once-shamed people. Where do you still carry old shame that God's covenant faithfulness says should already be behind you?
The Servant rejoices, clothed in garments of salvation like a bridegroom and bride. Does your own relationship with God feel like duty, or does it carry this same note of genuine celebration?

translationNotes.note: Luke 4:18 quotes this verse from the Greek Septuagint, which adds and recovery of sight to the blind, a phrase not present in the Hebrew Masoretic Text but consistent with the passage's themes of release and restoration.

Jesus' reading in Luke 4:19 stops at to proclaim the year of the LORD's favor, omitting and the day of our God's vengeance to comfort all who mourn, a deliberate, well-recognized break in the citation.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 61:1-11; no em dashes present in this passage. Sermon verified this session at thegospelcoalition.org.
